query: clinical features of anticholinergic toxicity including pupil dilation, hyperreflexia, delirium, and elevated heart rate

retrieved_docs.title: Sympathomimetic Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The early signs of sympathomimetic toxicity include tachycardia, mydriasis, hypertension, increased arousal or psychomotor agitation, and diaphoresis. As toxicity progresses, patients may develop increased muscle tone, hyperreflexia, delirium, hallucinations, and hyperthermia. Severe end-organ effects may include myocardial or cerebrovascular ischemia, intracranial hemorrhage, rhabdomyolysis, renal failure, coagulopathy, seizures, metabolic acidosis, and cardiovascular collapse. Thus, besides a thorough evaluation of cardiovascular and renal symptoms, the physical examination should also assess for neurologic damage that may manifest as abnormal mental status, pupil size, muscle tone, and reflexes.

retrieved_docs.title: Diphenhydramine Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with diphenhydramine toxicity present with an extension of adverse effects and generally include CNS depression and anticholinergic symptoms. Patients that present with mild to moderate poisoning exhibit more anticholinergic symptoms, which include dry mucous membranes, disorientation, tachycardia, mydriasis, urinary retention, hyperthermia, and decreased bowel sounds. Patients with more severe toxicity may also have severe delirium, seizures, psychosis, hypotension, dystonic reactions, hallucinations, and cardiac arrhythmias (i.e., QT prolongation and QRS prolongation). In rare cases, there are reports of rhabdomyolysis and renal failure in patients with prolonged agitation, coma, or seizures.

retrieved_docs.title: Diphenhydramine -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Diphenhydramine overdose can cause significant toxicity and result in various sequelae, including agitation, cardiac arrhythmias, rhabdomyolysis and the classic anticholinergic toxidrome. [39] [40] [41] [42] [41] [39] Signs and symptoms of a diphenhydramine overdose are listed below. Delirium Agitation Confusion Restlessness Hallucinations Ataxia Tremor Seizure Dry sweat glands and mucous membranes Flushed skin Elevated body temperature Mydriasis and blurry vision Urinary retention Tachycardia Rhabdomyolysis

retrieved_docs.title: Trihexyphenidyl -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Trihexyphenidyl toxicity resembles atropine intoxication (antimuscarinic effects) with xerostomia, anhidrosis, mydriasis, nausea/vomiting, tachycardia, hyperpyrexia, decreased bowel and bladder movements, rash, and hyperthermia, which usually accompany excessive doses. CNS symptoms observed with overdose include confusion, restlessness, agitation, incoordination, paranoid and psychotic reactions, delirium, and hallucinations. Reports exist of CNS depression leading to coma, respiratory and circulatory failure, and death in cases of severe overdose. [36] If the overdose does not receive prompt treatment, it could be fatal, especially in younger children. [10]

retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure Cardiovascular collapse Rhabdomyolysis Seizures Coma Permanent disability Death

retrieved_docs.content: Xerostomia (dry mouth) Central Nervous System Symptoms: Dizziness Confusion Agitation Delirium Ophthalmic Symptoms: Visual impairment, including blurred vision Mydriasis

retrieved_docs.title: Antiemetic Antimuscarinics -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: Tachycardia, lethargy, convulsion, visual disturbance, dry flushed skin, dry mouth, urinary retention, agitation, coma, supraventricular arrhythmias, confusion, and hallucinations are signs and symptoms of anticholinergic toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Atropine -- Toxicity -- Signs and Symptoms of Overdose
retrieved_docs.content: An overdose can lead to increased antimuscarinic adverse effects of dilated pupils, warm, dry skin, tachycardia, tremor, ataxia, delirium, and coma. In extreme toxicity, circulatory collapse secondary to respiratory failure may occur after paralysis and coma. Ten milligrams or less may be fatal to a child, while there is no known adult lethal dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Anticholinergic Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The clinician must be familiar with the anticholinergic toxidrome as a reliable history is not always obtainable. The mnemonic “red as a beet, dry as a bone, blind as a bat, mad as a hatter, hot as a hare, full as a flask” is helpful to remember the classic signs and symptoms of anticholinergic poisoning. Respectively, these include flushing, anhydrosis, dry mucous membranes, mydriasis, altered mental status, fever, and urinary retention. Decreased bowel sounds are also a common physical exam finding. Central nervous system effects may include delirium, hallucinations, agitation, restlessness, confusion, staccato speech, and picking at clothing and bedding. Seizures and jerking movements are possible. [22] [23] Diphenhydramine, in particular, has been reported to be associated with wide-complex tachycardia and QT prolongation. [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Anticholinergic Medications -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: At high dosages and approaching the range of anticholinergic toxicity, central adverse effects include signs of agitation, confusion, delirium, and seizures. [6] Drugs that more readily cross the blood-brain barrier tend to cause central nervous system adverse effects more frequently than those with limited ability to cross the border. [15] Common peripheral adverse effects of medications with anticholinergic activity are described below, organized by organ system: General: Hyperthermia and anhidrosis [6] Cardiovascular: Tachycardia, flushing, and arrhythmias [6] [16] Gastrointestinal: Reduced gut motility, constipation, vomiting, reduced saliva, and tear production Genitourinary: Urinary retention [6] [17] [18] HEENT: Blurred vision, mydriasis, narrow-angle glaucoma, and potentially vision loss [6] Skin: Inhibition of sweating [11] [19] Musculoskeletal: Diminished muscle contraction

retrieved_docs.title: Cataplexy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs) have fallen out of favor for the treatment of cataplexy due to their anticholinergic side effects (i.e., delirium, dry mucous membranes, hyperthermia, ileus, mydriasis, tachycardia, and urinary retention). Moreover, TCA overdose can result in a toxidrome: anticholinergic symptoms, cardiac toxicity, and CNS toxicity. Cardiac toxicity includes interventricular conduction delay (QRS prolongation), right-axis deviation, and tachycardia (anticholinergic effect). [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Sympathomimetic Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Antipsychotics may be considered in this setting when hallucinations or choreoathetosis is the predominant manifestation, suggesting excessive dopaminergic rather than adrenergic activity. The use of high-dose 2nd-generation antipsychotics may complicate the clinical picture by triggering anticholinergic delirium. Adjunctive medications such as the α2-receptor agonist dexmedetomidine may also be administered for persistent hypertension, tachycardia, and agitation.

retrieved_docs.title: Delirium Tremens(Archived) -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Seizures Disorientation Hypertension Hyperthermia Altered mental status Global confusion Arrhythmias Aspiration pneumonitis Respiratory failure Death

retrieved_docs.title: Anxiolytics and Sedative-Hypnotics Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Overdoses associated with SSRI/SNRIs rarely cause death or serious injury. [8] [9] [10] Nearly all fatal overdoses involve the co-ingestion of another substance or massive quantities. There is a possibility of serotonin syndrome, which results from the overstimulation of central and peripheral serotonin receptors. Generally, this presents in a patient with anxiety, agitation, delirium, diaphoresis, tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, gastrointestinal distress, tremor, muscle rigidity, myoclonus, and hyperreflexia. [9] Notably, bupropion and venlafaxine can precipitate seizures when ingested at toxic levels. Status epilepticus is rare in an overdose of these drugs. TCA overdose presents with mental status change, including sedation, confusion, delirium, or hallucination. There may be cardiac conduction delays, arrhythmias, hypotension, and anticholinergic toxicity (eg, hyperthermia, flushing, pupillary dilation.) Patients may initially present normally and then deteriorate rapidly due to the variable absorption kinetics involved in TCAs.

retrieved_docs.title: Excited Delirium (Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Hyper (hypertension/hyperthyroidism/hypercarbia/hyperthermia)

retrieved_docs.title: Diphenhydramine Toxicity -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis should include any substance or condition that may alter mentation, result in tachycardia, urinary retention, seizures, or other anticholinergic effects. Other organic processes should also be included, such as meningitis or sepsis. To help with ruling out causes, the time of onset of delirium is important. Toxin-induced delirium occurs more rapidly and abruptly than organic causes. In patients with altered mental status, hyperthermia, and agitation, salicylate toxicity should also be ruled out.

Collectively, these relationships illustrate a network in which anticholinergic toxicity, withdrawal‑related autonomic changes, post‑concussive sequelae, and age‑related organic mental disorders share overlapping clinical features such as confusion, hallucinations, tachycardia, and psychosis. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in differentiating and managing complex presentations that may involve anticholinergic effects, neuro‑traumatic aftermath, or degenerative psychiatric conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: The anticholinergic syndrome (متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ)) is a broad clinical entity that encompasses a variety of neuro‑psychiatric and autonomic manifestations. Within this syndrome, the construct “الهَذَيان” is a member and serves as the parent of “Behavioral/psychiatric manifestations,” indicating that behavioral and psychiatric symptoms are considered part of the anticholinergic effect profile. Additional components classified as members of the SMQ include “تسرع القلب الارتدادي” (rebound tachycardia), “حالة ارتباكية دون الحادة” (mild confusional state), and “متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية” (organic hallucination syndrome). The category “تأثيرات الانسحاب والرجوع” (withdrawal and rebound effects) has “تسرع القلب الارتدادي” as a child, linking withdrawal phenomena directly to the occurrence of rebound tachycardia within the anticholinergic framework. Separately, the diagnostic grouping “Senility and organic mental disorders” is classified under multiple overlapping

retrieved_docs.content: interrelated entities demonstrate how drug‑induced, medically‑caused, neurodegenerative, and age‑related psychiatric conditions intersect through shared diagnostic categories, overlapping symptom clusters (anxiety, mood disturbance, psychosis, confusional states, motor stereotypies, affective blunting), and common neurobiological mechanisms such as brain atrophy and cholinergic dysfunction, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 238
retrieved_docs.content: Late effect of nervous system injury is identified as متلازمة ما بعد الارتجاج المخي, and this post‑concussion syndrome is also used to classify the broader category of Senility and organic mental disorders. Senility and organic mental disorders encompass a wide spectrum of neuropsychiatric conditions, including schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders, حالة ارتباكية دون الحادة (a sub‑acute confusional state), خرف وعائي مصحوب حالة مزاجية اكتئابية (vascular dementia with depressive mood), ذُهان شيخوخي (senile psychosis), ضمور الدماغ المحدد (focal brain atrophy), and متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية (organic hallucination syndrome). The sub‑acute confusional state, متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية, and تسرع القلب الارتدادي (recurrent tachycardia) are all members of متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ), a cholinergic‑antagonist syndrome. Within this framework, withdrawal and rebound effects (تأثيرات الانسحاب والرجوع) can give rise to تسرع القلب الارتدادي, linking pharmacologic rebound phenomena to the SMQ cluster. Focal brain atrophy (ضمور الدماغ المحدد) manifests clinically as حركات متكررة نمطية (stereotyped repetitive movements) and تبلد المشاعر (emotional blunting). Notably, genderová identita (gender identity) is reported to have a relationship with حركات متكررة نمطية, suggesting that alterations in gender identity may be associated with the repetitive motor patterns that arise from focal cerebral atrophy. Overall, these interconnections illustrate how late nervous‑system injury, age‑related organic mental disorders, specific neuropsychiatric syndromes, cholinergic‑antagonist toxicity, and certain psychosocial factors converge on shared clinical features such as confusional states, psychosis, hallucinations, motor stereotypies, emotional blunting, and autonomic disturbances, underscoring the importance of integrated assessment and management in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2295
retrieved_docs.content: The subacute confusional state (حالة ارتباكية دون الحادة) and the organic hallucinosis syndrome (متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية) are both classified as members of the cholinergic antagonist syndrome (متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ)). This categorization indicates that these clinical presentations are recognized manifestations associated with exposure to cholinergic antagonists, and grouping them under the SMQ facilitates systematic identification, monitoring, and management of adverse effects related to cholinergic blockade in patient care.

Miscellaneous mental‑health disorders are broadly related to an unspecified transient organic mental disorder and are primarily mapped from organic mood disorder, indicating an organic substrate for diverse psychiatric presentations. Within this group of nonspecific clinical manifestations, the specific finding of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse) is recognized as a child element, illustrating that severe cardiovascular failure can be one of the many possible presentations encompassed by ill‑defined symptom complexes. In addition, حالة ارتباكية دون الحادة and متلازمة الهلوسة العضوية are also identified as members of متلازمة مُضَادّات الفِعْلِ الكولينيّ (SMQ). Together, these entities illustrate that the anticholinergic syndrome (SMQ) encompasses a range of related conditions, including الهَذَيان, a non‑acute confusional state, and an organic hallucinosis syndrome, all of which can present with behavioral and psychiatric features.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 603
retrieved_docs.content: Syncope, which is classified as a type of loss of consciousness (فقدان الوعي), is clinically associated with chest constriction (خُنَاقٌ صَدْرِيّ). Chest constriction is also associated with spinal rigidity (تَصَلُّبٌ شِرْيَانِيّ). These interconnections indicate that patients experiencing syncope may report a sensation of tightness in the chest, and that spinal rigidity may be linked to the same chest symptom, underscoring the importance of evaluating both thoracic and spinal findings when assessing episodes of loss of consciousness.
